display_title: 1989-03_04 — How To Participate in the Centennial Games
body_html:
Page 1

BEARING 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
March-April 1989

How To Participate in the Centennial Games

The Washington Centennial Games will take place in Spokane this summer from August 10-13. The Centennial Games organizers have divided the state into 8 zones, from which 50 official Games competitors will be selected. Each zone has a representative, who is responsible for making sure that all orienteers in his/her zone know about the Centennial Games and how to participate. There will three categories for both men and women: Junior (13-20); Open; and Masters (over 35).

Qualifying Meets
Because we are allowed only 50 official competitors, four meets have been designated to select participants:

Bridle Trails Park, February 25 (Juniors only) Easton Grade, June 4
Pt. Defiance Park, June 10 Drunken Charlie Lake, June 17

At each qualifying meet, the top 2 finishers in each category will automatically qualify for the Centennial Games. If additional competitors are needed, the 8 zone representatives, the State Chairman, and the Meet Director will select them, with the goal of ensuring that participants represent a wide range of ages and geographical origins. The zone representatives, State Chairman, and Meet Director are listed below with their phone numbers. If you have any questions about how to participate in the Games, please call any of them.

[Map of Washington State divided into 8 numbered zones]

COC President Resigns

Arnold Kay, newly-elected COC president, has resigned his position due to job commitments. Although his term as president has been short, Arnold has been the club’s equipment coordinator for several years, and has contributed his time to Cascade in many other ways. We appreciate the time and effort he has spent, and hope his job won’t keep him from participating in meets.

Of course, this means that the office of President is open to anyone who wants it. If you are interested in taking over as President, please call Doug Sprugel at 365-8742.

Nutrition Talk March 22

Reserve your evening Wednesday, March 22 for an exciting, enlightening, and informative talk on Nutritional Intervention! Guest speaker Francis DiBiase will be at the University Branch Library (50th & Roosevelt, Seattle) to talk about how nutrition affects athletes, particularly orienteers. Fran will also touch upon micro/macro nutrition and overall diet structure. As an added bonus, he will discuss the immune system and how to cope with stress. So, come one, come all. Bring your friends and neighbors and all your questions for Fran.

Military Unit, Address, and Post/Zip Code:

FEES: $20.00/person for first two members; $10.00/person for added persons; Free to kids under 12 in group team. We need your early registration, so a $10.00 late fee per team applies to all who register after APRIL FIRST. Make your payments in US dollars or fair equivalent by check payable to WSOA, P.O. Box 111977, Tacoma, Wa 98411-1977. Write for further info here.
Fees include hot and cold food served from about 3 PM Saturday thru 11:30 AM Sunday, 2 maps and descriptions per team, eligibility for awards and draw prizes - plus a great, challenging ROGAINE! If you enclose a self-addressed large envelope, we’ll send upon receipt of this form and your fees a confirmation and package of information and sample courses.

CANCELLATION AND CHANGES IN ENTRY: A fee of $5.00 per person will be charged for any cancellation made before May 1st, after which no refunds can be given. Name changes are permitted until the 8:00 AM Saturday on-site check-in and map issue.
All members of each team must sign a Waiver Form at the base camp site before embarking on the course. It releases WSOA, sponsors, and the landowners from any responsibility for risks and hazards on the course. You may obtain a copy in advance if you so request. Please read and heed the recommendations for equipment to take along, which you’ll get upon registration.

Page 4

EVENT DESCRIPTION: This First ROGAINE to be held in the United States is a part of the Centennial celebration for our State. Entrants will gather at a base camp in the Cascade Mountains near Cle Elum either Friday night or Saturday morning to await the issuance of the maps and course descriptions promptly at 8 AM. Teams then have two hours to study their maps, choose routes and decide on needed supplies, and otherwise prepare. At 10:00 AM all teams begin their navigation to as many pre-set controls at locations shown on the map during the 12 or 24 hour period of their event. Teams may return to the camp at any time to eat, rest, or socialize at the campfire.

To win in your category, you try to amass the most points by reaching as many controls as possible, including some high-point ones way out/up/down there, in the Wenatchee Forest covering some 100++ square kilometers of terrain. There will be at least 40 controls plus one mystery control to locate. Most teams will find the competition secondary to the experience of doing long-distance navigating in great surrounding, including perhaps some night map and compass work. The wisest ones probably will head for the campfire about dark and enjoy the comradeship and rest, then watch the 10 PM finish for the 12-hour, and then get up if they must to finish off a few more controls the next morning. The hard-core ROGAINERS will first be seen again about 10 minutes before the finish time, running/dragging themselves in to avoid the outrageously high penalty points for being late. No one will separate from their partner(s) during the event, since this means instant disqualification.

The team categories allow for many approaches to this event: the MEN, WOMEN, AND MASTERS teams can be expected to have many runners/orienteers who will score high; some MIXED and NOVICE/FAMILY/GROUP teams will be out just for the fun of it; and many will choose to take full advantage of the food and base camp social scene. Most will try some night navigation.

The MAP will be a USGS 2-color 1:24,000 scale with 40 foot contours (yes - those squiggly lines that spell DOOM to those out of shape!) The map with pre-marked control, plus a description sheet will be issued to all registrants at 8 AM on Saturday, May 13th at the base camp (note to Aussies - that’s the Hash House!) Late registrants mark their map from a master map.

The official start is 10 AM, so you have two hours to plan (overestimate) the number of control locations you’ll go for, and how you’ll get there. At each control you’ll find a regular Orienteering White/Orange nylon bag, plus a sign-in log sheet where you report your arrival time, team number, and where you intend to head next. These log sheets could be of immense value later to the Search and Rescue folks in case you don’t come back at the Finish time (10 PM for the 12-hour, 10 AM Sunday for the 24-hour).

Food and water will be available thru most of the event, and the awards ceremony and drawing for prizes for those who got back and are still around will begin about 11:00 AM on Sunday. If you’re a 12-hour ROGAINE winner, and have to leave that night, we’ll mail your award.

You’ll get more information in your registration packet, but for now plan to bring and carry with you appropriate clothing for unpredictable mountain weather conditions (from high 80s to low 40s in a few hours), basic survival and first aid kits, whistle (a loud one) and a compass you know intimately! You must also carry emergency food and water, plus water purification means in case you can’t reach our water points. The fairly central base camp will have space for tents, nearby car parking, toilets, and drinking water but no running water. You may set up camp and loop out and back several times from here, or take gear for an overnight anywhere on the map. This is a primitive area, so expect to see wildlife, but bring no pets of your own. Precautions against fire, contamination, or littering of our National Forest must be taken by all.

For further information, contact:
WASHINGTON STATE ORIENTEERING ASSOCIATION
PO BOX 111977
Tacoma, WA 98411-1977

Baring My Bearings

Am I nearer
or farther
from where I should be
The miles I've used
are not amused
I traveled haphazardly
The precious time
our daily day
and night just slip away
I haven't reached
my future yet
my map won't read that way
Place myself
within the grid
familiar surroundings please
I'm not fatigued
or lost just yet
I see locks but have no keys
Turn left then north
or right then south
are they one and the same
It's funny how
my lifestyle seems
so much like this game
The clock says go
and I begin
until the race is done
If only I
could draw the map
the way I want to run

-Roger Stocker

Matchmaker, Matchmaker

Would you like to participate in the ROGAINE on May 13-14, but just can’t find the right partner? Dave Lilly may have the solution to your problem. Just let him know what you’re looking for in a ROGAINE partner and he will try to match you with the ROGAINEr of your dreams. Call Dave now at 454-4136.

1989 Western States Orienteering Championships

September 9-10
Moran State Park, Orcas Island

look for details in future issues of "Bearing 315"

Ellensburg Elects Officers

Bearing 315’s Eastern Slope Correspondent, Ellensburg Orienteering Club’s Dale Width, reports that EOC has elected its 1989 officers. Serving this year are: Betty Wiberg, president; Dale Width, vice president and event chair; Addie Graaf, secretary; and Sally Evans, treasurer.

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we’ll start with positive roster changes as of 25 February 1989. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Betty Berreth family, Rick & Eileen Breseman, Paul Bullard, Ardis Dull, Scott Elich, Dave Enger family, Jim Gallant & Deb Danner, John Huth (yes, even though he is living in Nevada), Kent Jameson, Ken Knapp & Katherine Schubert-Knapp, Steve Layman & Laura Morin, Ken Lew, Amy Loomis family, Matti Löppönen (yes, even though he is living in Georgia), Bruce McAlister, Jim McNamara, Robert & Sandy Kish Miller, Carl Moore, Debbie Newell & Arnold Kay, Dot Newell, Robert Olsen, Jan Vrana, Dan Waugh, Melanie Wheeler, Ken Wiljanen, and Bob Williamson.

If you all don’t come to meets, you’ll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops.
Now on to the fun stuff. Unfortunately, I couldn't come up with any for this issue. Since I am so involved with World Cup 1990, I don’t seem to be talking to as many folks as I used to, so I need help with compiling items of interest for this column. If you want more news about people in the club, you’ll have to write something or be sure to tell me something that is for this column.

Event Key
A - major event on good quality color map, 5 courses minimum
B - major local event, 3-4 courses minimum
C - local club event, 3 or 4 courses
S - special event (bicycle, canoe, ski, night, etc.)
Q - Centennial Games qualifier (everyone welcome)

Club Key
COC - Cascade O Club; EOC - Ellensburg O Club; EWOC - Eastern Washington O Club; NISQ - Nisqually Orienteers; SAMM - Sammamish O Club; WSOA - Washington State O Assoc. BAOC - Bay Area O Club; CVO - Cowichan Valley Orienteers; GVOC - Greater Vancouver O Club; SLOC - St. Louis O Club; SOCK - Sage O Club of Kamloops; VO - VictOrienteers

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club March-April 1989
How To Participate in the Centennial Games

The Washington Centennial Games will take place in Spokane this summer from August 10-13. The Centennial Games organizers have divided the state into 8 zones, from which 50 official Games competitors will be selected. Each zone has a representative, who is responsible for making sure that all orienteers in his/her zone know about the Centennial Games and how to participate. There will three categories for both men and women: Junior (13-20); Open; and Masters (over 35).
Qualifying Meets Because we are allowed only 50 official competitors, four meets have been designated to select participants:
Bridle Trails Park, February 25 (Juniors only) Easton Grade, June 4 Pt. Defiance Park, June 10 Drunken Charlie Lake, June 17

At each qualifying meet, the top 2 finishers in each category will automatically qualify for the Centennial Games. If additional competitors are needed, the 8 zone representatives, the State Chairman, and the Meet Director will select them, with the goal of ensuring that participants represent a wide range of ages and geographical origins. The zone representatives, State Chairman, and Meet Director are listed below with their phone numbers. If you have any questions about how to participate in the Games, please call any of them.
[Map of Washington State divided into 8 numbered zones]

Arnold Kay, newly-elected COC president, has resigned his position due to job commitments. Although his term as president has been short, Arnold has been the club’s equipment coordinator for several years, and has contributed his time to Cascade in many other ways. We appreciate the time and effort he has spent, and hope his job won’t keep him from participating in meets.
Of course, this means that the office of President is open to anyone who wants it. If you are interested in taking over as President, please call Doug Sprugel at 365-8742.

Reserve your evening Wednesday, March 22 for an exciting, enlightening, and informative talk on Nutritional Intervention! Guest speaker Francis DiBiase will be at the University Branch Library (50th & Roosevelt, Seattle) to talk about how nutrition affects athletes, particularly orienteers. Fran will also touch upon micro/macro nutrition and overall diet structure. As an added bonus, he will discuss the immune system and how to cope with stress. So, come one, come all. Bring your friends and neighbors and all your questions for Fran.

ROGAINE Team Entry Form - ROGAINE USA-89 near Cle Elum, Washington State
First team member listed is team contact for all mailings:
Check your team category: Men 18+ ( ) Women ( ) Masters 40+ ( ) Novice/Family/Group ( ) - oldest age = \\\
If military team competing in Adventure Challenge, fill in: Military Unit, Address, and Post/Zip Code:

FEES: $20.00/person for first two members; $10.00/person for added persons; Free to kids under 12 in group team. We need your early registration, so a $10.00 late fee per team applies to all who register after APRIL FIRST. Make your payments in US dollars or fair equivalent by check payable to WSOA, P.O. Box 111977, Tacoma, Wa 98411-1977. Write for further info here. Fees include hot and cold food served from about 3 PM Saturday thru 11:30 AM Sunday, 2 maps and descriptions per team, eligibility for awards and draw prizes - plus a great, challenging ROGAINE! If you enclose a self-addressed large envelope, we’ll send upon receipt of this form and your fees a confirmation and package of information and sample courses.

CANCELLATION AND CHANGES IN ENTRY: A fee of $5.00 per person will be charged for any cancellation made before May 1st, after which no refunds can be given. Name changes are permitted until the 8:00 AM Saturday on-site check-in and map issue. All members of each team must sign a Waiver Form at the base camp site before embarking on the course. It releases WSOA, sponsors, and the landowners from any responsibility for risks and hazards on the course. You may obtain a copy in advance if you so request. Please read and heed the recommendations for equipment to take along, which you’ll get upon registration.

EVENT DESCRIPTION: This First ROGAINE to be held in the United States is a part of the Centennial celebration for our State. Entrants will gather at a base camp in the Cascade Mountains near Cle Elum either Friday night or Saturday morning to await the issuance of the maps and course descriptions promptly at 8 AM. Teams then have two hours to study their maps, choose routes and decide on needed supplies, and otherwise prepare. At 10:00 AM all teams begin their navigation to as many pre-set controls at locations shown on the map during the 12 or 24 hour period of their event. Teams may return to the camp at any time to eat, rest, or socialize at the campfire.

To win in your category, you try to amass the most points by reaching as many controls as possible, including some high-point ones way out/up/down there, in the Wenatchee Forest covering some 100++ square kilometers of terrain. There will be at least 40 controls plus one mystery control to locate. Most teams will find the competition secondary to the experience of doing long-distance navigating in great surrounding, including perhaps some night map and compass work. The wisest ones probably will head for the campfire about dark and enjoy the comradeship and rest, then watch the 10 PM finish for the 12-hour, and then get up if they must to finish off a few more controls the next morning. The hard-core ROGAINERS will first be seen again about 10 minutes before the finish time, running/dragging themselves in to avoid the outrageously high penalty points for being late. No one will separate from their partner(s) during the event, since this means instant disqualification.

The team categories allow for many approaches to this event: the MEN, WOMEN, AND MASTERS teams can be expected to have many runners/orienteers who will score high; some MIXED and NOVICE/FAMILY/GROUP teams will be out just for the fun of it; and many will choose to take full advantage of the food and base camp social scene. Most will try some night navigation.

The MAP will be a USGS 2-color 1:24,000 scale with 40 foot contours (yes - those squiggly lines that spell DOOM to those out of shape!) The map with pre-marked control, plus a description sheet will be issued to all registrants at 8 AM on Saturday, May 13th at the base camp (note to Aussies - that’s the Hash House!) Late registrants mark their map from a master map.

The official start is 10 AM, so you have two hours to plan (overestimate) the number of control locations you’ll go for, and how you’ll get there. At each control you’ll find a regular Orienteering White/Orange nylon bag, plus a sign-in log sheet where you report your arrival time, team number, and where you intend to head next. These log sheets could be of immense value later to the Search and Rescue folks in case you don’t come back at the Finish time (10 PM for the 12-hour, 10 AM Sunday for the 24-hour).

Food and water will be available thru most of the event, and the awards ceremony and drawing for prizes for those who got back and are still around will begin about 11:00 AM on Sunday. If you’re a 12-hour ROGAINE winner, and have to leave that night, we’ll mail your award.

You’ll get more information in your registration packet, but for now plan to bring and carry with you appropriate clothing for unpredictable mountain weather conditions (from high 80s to low 40s in a few hours), basic survival and first aid kits, whistle (a loud one) and a compass you know intimately! You must also carry emergency food and water, plus water purification means in case you can’t reach our water points. The fairly central base camp will have space for tents, nearby car parking, toilets, and drinking water but no running water. You may set up camp and loop out and back several times from here, or take gear for an overnight anywhere on the map. This is a primitive area, so expect to see wildlife, but bring no pets of your own. Precautions against fire, contamination, or littering of our National Forest must be taken by all.
For further information, contact: WASHINGTON STATE ORIENTEERING ASSOCIATION PO BOX 111977 Tacoma, WA 98411-1977

Am I nearer or farther from where I should be The miles I've used are not amused I traveled haphazardly The precious time our daily day and night just slip away I haven't reached my future yet my map won't read that way Place myself within the grid familiar surroundings please I'm not fatigued or lost just yet I see locks but have no keys Turn left then north or right then south are they one and the same It's funny how my lifestyle seems so much like this game The clock says go and I begin until the race is done If only I could draw the map the way I want to run

Would you like to participate in the ROGAINE on May 13-14, but just can’t find the right partner? Dave Lilly may have the solution to your problem. Just let him know what you’re looking for in a ROGAINE partner and he will try to match you with the ROGAINEr of your dreams. Call Dave now at 454-4136.
September 9-10 Moran State Park, Orcas Island
look for details in future issues of "Bearing 315"

Bearing 315’s Eastern Slope Correspondent, Ellensburg Orienteering Club’s Dale Width, reports that EOC has elected its 1989 officers. Serving this year are: Betty Wiberg, president; Dale Width, vice president and event chair; Addie Graaf, secretary; and Sally Evans, treasurer.

As usual, we’ll start with positive roster changes as of 25 February 1989. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Betty Berreth family, Rick & Eileen Breseman, Paul Bullard, Ardis Dull, Scott Elich, Dave Enger family, Jim Gallant & Deb Danner, John Huth (yes, even though he is living in Nevada), Kent Jameson, Ken Knapp & Katherine Schubert-Knapp, Steve Layman & Laura Morin, Ken Lew, Amy Loomis family, Matti Löppönen (yes, even though he is living in Georgia), Bruce McAlister, Jim McNamara, Robert & Sandy Kish Miller, Carl Moore, Debbie Newell & Arnold Kay, Dot Newell, Robert Olsen, Jan Vrana, Dan Waugh, Melanie Wheeler, Ken Wiljanen, and Bob Williamson.

If you all don’t come to meets, you’ll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. Now on to the fun stuff. Unfortunately, I couldn't come up with any for this issue. Since I am so involved with World Cup 1990, I don’t seem to be talking to as many folks as I used to, so I need help with compiling items of interest for this column. If you want more news about people in the club, you’ll have to write something or be sure to tell me something that is for this column.
